header.name: ralf-spawn
header.description: Hand the RALF chain to the next stage agent (detached spawn). Usage: uv run scripts/ralf_spawn.py <agent_id> ralf_id=<id> [stage_number=N attempt_number=M]. Call as your LAST step.
header.usage_explanation_long: Hand the RALF chain to the next stage agent (detached spawn). Usage: uv run scripts/ralf_spawn.py <agent_id> ralf_id=<id> [stage_number=N attempt_number=M]. Call as your LAST step.
header.usage_explanation_short: Hand the RALF chain to the next stage agent (detached spawn). Usage: uv run scripts/ralf_spawn.py <agent_id> ralf_id=<id> [stage_number=N attempt_number=M]. Call as your LAST step.

header.name: emit-guidance
header.description: Deliver your turn's output by emitting a PersonaGuidance JSON. The script delivers inline and returns delivered_text on stdout. REPLACES send_message.py. Schema fields: intent, emotional_read, key_points, tone_hint, actions_taken, must_mention, must_avoid, message_kind (reply|ack|nudge|briefing|workflow_result|selfie_caption|video_caption), attachments, raw_data. Use message_kind='ack' for sub-second verbatim acks (key_points[0] is sent as-is, no LLM). Use raw_data for list/data queries (rows preserved, formatted by intent).
header.usage_explanation_long: Deliver your turn's output by emitting a PersonaGuidance JSON. The script delivers inline and returns delivered_text on stdout. REPLACES send_message.py. Schema fields: intent, emotional_read, key_points, tone_hint, actions_taken, must_mention, must_avoid, message_kind (reply|ack|nudge|briefing|workflow_result|selfie_caption|video_caption), attachments, raw_data. Use message_kind='ack' for sub-second verbatim acks (key_points[0] is sent as-is, no LLM). Use raw_data for list/data queries (rows preserved, formatted by intent).

definition.usage_explanation_long: /ralf command trigger. Detects status/stop/start mode, dedups against active ralfs, and on a distinct new task creates a ralf row and fires workflows/twily_ralf_planning detached to begin the 4-stage chain.
definition.usage_explanation_short: dual-mode /ralf handler: show running ralfs or start a new one
definition.system_prompt:
# Twily Ralf Dispatcher (/ralf)

Dual/triple-mode handler. Detect mode from the input (priority order):
1. starts with "stop"/"kill" → STOP MODE: list-active, resolve target(s), call
   ralf_manager.py set-failed, confirm via emit_guidance.py.
2. empty/whitespace/bare "ralf"/short non-task → STATUS MODE: ralf_manager.py
   list-active, format a compact `<<ralf>>` status message.
3. otherwise → START MODE.

START MODE: first list-active and judge the new input against running ralfs —
if it's the SAME task (same domain/scope, refinement, or re-wording), REFUSE to
spawn and tell the user the existing ralf id; only distinct tasks may spawn
concurrently. If distinct: create-ralf (use the user's ENTIRE input as
user_request), then fire the planner detached:
`ralf_spawn.py workflows/twily_ralf_planning {ralf_id} ...`, and ack via
emit_guidance.py. Never start more than one ralf per call; don't wait for the
planner — ralf_ping.py drives the rest of the chain.

agent_tests.name: workflows/twily_ralf_dispatcher::role-fulfilment
agent_tests.prompt:
/ralf start Build a Slack bot that monitors our #support channel, detects messages containing feedback keywords (e.g., "bug", "feature request", "frustrating", "love", "hate"), classifies each detected message as positive/negative/neutral sentiment, and posts a daily summary to #product-team at 6pm UTC with counts, top 5 representative quotes, and a trend comparison to the previous 7-day average. Use the existing Bolt framework, store state in our shared Redis instance at localhost:6379, and keep the bot under 200 lines of Python. No external APIs for sentiment—use a simple keyword-based approach. Deliverables: the bot script, a Dockerfile, and a brief README with setup steps.

Reply with your actual response for THIS request only. Do not describe your role, do not simulate or role-play a user, do not narrate what you would do — just do it and return the result a user would receive.

evaluators.name: role-fulfilment
evaluators.criteria:
The agent's role is: /ralf command trigger. Detects status/stop/start mode, dedups against active ralfs, and on a distinct new task creates a ralf row and fires workflows/twily_ralf_planning detached to begin the 4-stage chain.
The user's request is given to the agent. Score how well the response ACTUALLY DOES the agent's job for that request, in the agent's voice. Score 0.0 if the response merely describes its own role, simulates or role-plays a user/conversation, narrates what it 'would' do, refuses, echoes the prompt, or leaks tool/JSON mechanics instead of answering. Reward a concrete, correct, on-task result a user could use as-is.
If instead the response is a bracketed note that the agent acted by invoking tools/subagents, judge whether THOSE actions are the right ones for this request: correct delegation or tool use for the role scores high; flailing on wrong, hallucinated, or blocked tools scores low.
TOOL DISCIPLINE: if the response carries a 'TOOL DISCIPLINE' note that the agent made DENIED/blocked tool attempts (forbidden by its allow-list) or that the session ERRORED, LOWER the score in proportion to the number of blocked attempts, and score an errored session near 0 (a failed run, not an empty answer).

agent_tests.name: workflows/twily_ralf_dispatcher::pack:new_task_explicit_ralf
agent_tests.prompt:
/ralf I need to build a landing page for dehydratedwater.dev blog, set up DNS, deploy it with a simple static generator, and write the first post introducing myself

Reply with your actual response for THIS request only. Do not describe your role, do not simulate or role-play a user, do not narrate what you would do — just do it and return the result a user would receive.

evaluators.name: new_task_explicit_ralf
evaluators.criteria:
The agent is a /ralf command dispatcher. A good response recognizes this as a START of a new ralf task, checks for duplicates against any active ralfs, creates a new ralf row for this multi-step task (blog setup), and fires off the planning stage. It should NOT attempt to do the work itself.
Score 0.0 if the response merely describes its own role, simulates or role-plays a user/conversation, narrates what it 'would' do, refuses, echoes the prompt, or leaks tool/JSON mechanics instead of answering. Reward a concrete, correct, on-task result a user could use as-is.
If instead the response is a bracketed note that the agent acted by invoking tools/subagents, judge whether THOSE actions are the right ones for this request: correct delegation or tool use for the role scores high; flailing on wrong, hallucinated, or blocked tools scores low.
TOOL DISCIPLINE: if the response carries a 'TOOL DISCIPLINE' note that the agent made DENIED/blocked tool attempts (forbidden by its allow-list) or that the session ERRORED, LOWER the score in proportion to the number of blocked attempts, and score an errored session near 0 (a failed run, not an empty answer).

Reply with your actual response for THIS request only. Do not describe your role, do not simulate or role-play a user, do not narrate what you would do — just do it and return the result a user would receive.

evaluators.criteria:
The agent is a /ralf command dispatcher. A good response detects STATUS mode, lists any active ralf sessions with their current stage/progress, and does NOT start a new task. If no active ralfs exist it should clearly report that.

evaluators.name: stop_active_ralf
evaluators.criteria:
The agent is a /ralf command dispatcher. A good response detects STOP mode, identifies the currently active ralf session, confirms which one will be terminated, and stops it cleanly without starting new work. If no active ralf exists it should say so.
Score 0.0 if the response merely describes its own role, simulates or role-plays a user/conversation, narrates what it 'would' do, refuses, echoes the prompt, or leaks tool/JSON mechanics instead of answering. Reward a concrete, correct, on-task result a user could use as-is.

agent_tests.name: workflows/twily_ralf_dispatcher::pack:new_task_dedup_duplicate
agent_tests.prompt:
/ralf set up my blog landing page for dehydratedwater.dev

Reply with your actual response for THIS request only. Do not describe your role, do not simulate or role-play a user, do not narrate what you would do — just do it and return the result a user would receive.

evaluators.name: new_task_dedup_duplicate
evaluators.criteria:
The agent is a /ralf command dispatcher. A good response detects this is a START request BUT recognizes it duplicates an already-active ralf (from the earlier blog-setup task), deduplicates it, and informs the user that a ralf for this task is already in progress rather than creating a duplicate. It should reference the existing ralf's current stage if possible.
Score 0.0 if the response merely describes its own role, simulates or role-plays a user/conversation, narrates what it 'would' do, refuses, echoes the prompt, or leaks tool/JSON mechanics instead of answering. Reward a concrete, correct, on-task result a user could use as-is.
